document.title: Spark Capture Attention Layer
document.purpose: Describe an attention-capture layer for Spark Capture: a system that preserves many sparks while governing which sparks are allowed to draw active attention, energy, and implementation effort.
author: Lion Kimbro
Wing-Cat (ChatGPT)

core-insight.summary: The Spark list is not the attention system itself. Spark Capture records possible futures; the Attention Layer governs which of those futures currently have permission to claim focus.
core-insight.key-phrase: Spark catches possibility. Attention governs permission.
core-insight.metaphor: Lion is a 24/7 possibility reactor. Spark Capture is the holding chamber for sparks; the Attention Layer is the reactor vessel, control rod, and routing mechanism that turns possibility into directed work.

problem.description: A person with many active ideas can become captured by whichever spark feels most vivid in the moment. The danger is not having too many ideas; the danger is when a spark silently promotes itself into command without being consciously chosen.
symptoms: Every tool could become a world.
Every world could become a civilization.
The maker feels unable to stop because each possibility appears immediately buildable.
problem.design-challenge: Create a lightweight mechanism that lets sparks be honored and preserved without allowing all of them to become active work.

layer-model.principle: Separate durable idea capture from temporary attention state.

layers.name: spark-registry
layers.question: What ideas exist?
layers.responsibility: Store durable spark records by GUID. Sparks remain valid whether or not they are currently active.

layers.name: attention-layer
layers.question: What ideas are allowed to pull on me right now?
layers.responsibility: Store ordered references to spark GUIDs and assign each referenced spark an attention role.

layers.name: work-session-log
layers.question: What am I doing in this sitting?
layers.responsibility: Record the active session, current intention, notes, interruptions, decisions, and next return point.

layers.name: project-folder
layers.question: Where does implementation live?
layers.responsibility: Contain the actual files, code, artifacts, and project-specific records for work that has graduated from spark into build.

attention-stack.description: The Attention Layer may be represented as an ordered stack of spark GUID references. The order represents current attention priority, not permanent importance.
attention-stack.rule: Only one spark should occupy the active forge position at a time.

roles.meaning: The one active build. This spark is allowed to draw implementation energy now.
A spark that may be consulted or advanced only insofar as it directly supports the forge.
A spark that is emotionally or intellectually loud, but not currently actionable.
A captured spark that is explicitly not allowed to draw active work today.

attention-capture-mechanism.description: When a spark tries to seize attention, the system should make the promotion explicit. Capturing attention is not the same as starting work.
attention-capture-mechanism.trigger: The user notices that a spark is pulling attention, energy, urgency, or emotional charge.
attention-capture-mechanism.ritual-question: Why is this trying to capture attention?

smallest-safe-capture-options.meaning: Append the thought to the spark without changing its attention status.
Acknowledge the spark and explicitly mark it as not active.
Allow the spark to support the current forge, but not displace it.
Make the spark the one active build, displacing the previous forge through an explicit decision.

attention-capture-mechanism.control-phrase: Yes, I heard the idea. No, it does not get to steal the steering wheel.

stack.next-action: Define the minimal shipwright-01 JSON shape.

stack.reason: Useful only insofar as it supports the active forge.
stack.next-action: Capture supporting checklist items without implementing them yet.

stack.reason: Emotionally loud but not actionable today.
stack.next-action: Revisit during review, not during the active work session.

parking-lot.reason: Good idea, but not allowed to draw heat today.

recommended-files.purpose: Durable registry of sparks by GUID.
Current ordered references to spark GUIDs, representing the attention stack.
Current work-session state, including intention, active task, interruptions, and return point.
Optional daily attention and work log.

file-architecture.design-note: The attention file should reference sparks by GUID rather than duplicating spark content. This keeps spark identity stable while allowing attention state to change quickly.

spark-list: Indicate attention role with a small badge or lane marker.
Allow sorting by title, status, date, or current attention rank.

capture-attention-button.label: Capture Attention
capture-attention-button.behavior: Open a small dialog that asks why the spark is pulling attention and what the smallest safe capture should be.

attention-stack-view: Show the current forge at the top.
Show watchers separately from parked sparks.
Make it visually obvious that only one spark is the active forge.

fields.name: attention-reason
fields.prompt: Why is this trying to capture attention?

fields.name: smallest-safe-capture
fields.prompt: What is the smallest safe capture?

forge.reason: This appears to be the active framework project and the strongest candidate for the current forge.

support.reason: This may support the forge by making project activation faster, but should not become the main build unless explicitly promoted.

watchers.reason: This is strategically meaningful and emotionally bright, but may become a distraction if it pulls attention before there is work to point toward.
This is directly relevant to the attention problem, but should be captured as theory or design support rather than becoming a separate active project today.

parked.reason: Delightful and possibly valuable, but not part of the active forge.

operating-principles.explanation: The system should welcome unlimited possibility while protecting implementation from fragmentation.
Writing down a spark or adding it to the attention layer does not mean work has begun.
A spark should not become active work merely because it is vivid, exciting, or emotionally loud.
A support spark is allowed to draw attention only when it directly helps the active forge.
A parked spark is not rejected. It is preserved without being allowed to seize the steering wheel.
The goal is not to reduce excitement. The goal is to route excitement into finished artifacts.

aphorisms: I am allowed infinite sparks, but only one active forge.
A spark may be heard without being obeyed.
The dangerous moment is when a spark silently promotes itself into command.
Spark catches possibility. Attention governs permission.
Yes, I heard the idea. No, it does not get to steal the steering wheel.
open-questions: Should attention roles live only in attention.json, or should the spark registry cache the current role for display speed?
Should attention changes be logged historically?
Should the system support multiple named attention stacks, such as work, home, spiritual, writing, and business?
Should the attention stack be global, per-day, or per-work-session?
Should a parked spark have a review date?
Should promoting a new forge require explicitly demoting the old forge?
next-actions: Add GUIDs to all spark records if they do not already have them.
Create an attention.json file that references spark GUIDs.
Add a visible attention lane or badge to the Spark UI.
Add a Capture Attention button to the spark detail view.
Implement the smallest possible attention-stack view: Forge, Support, Watcher, Parked.
Do not build a complicated productivity system before the basic spark registry and attention stack are working.
